title: Discharging a Medically Complex Child: Family-Centered Care Coordination
subtitle: A 2-year-old is going home with new gastrostomy feeds and low-flow oxygen after a long hospitalization. The nurse coordinates an interdisciplinary, family-centered discharge — verifying caregiver competency and arranging equipment, home health, and follow-up.

patient.name: Noah Castillo
patient.admitDate: Pediatric unit, recovering from a prolonged respiratory illness. Now stable on low-flow home oxygen and tolerating gastrostomy (G-tube) feeds with weight gain. Preparing for a coordinated, family-centered discharge home.

vitals.temp: Parents practicing care; return demo pending
Two caregivers competent; DME + home health set

labs.v: RT, dietitian, social work, home health.

labs.r: What to do + when to call.

nursesNote: Noah Castillo, 2, medically complex, going home on low-flow oxygen with new G-tube feeds after a prolonged illness. Family-centered discharge: parents are partners and must be trained with teach-back and return demonstration; at least two caregivers should be competent. Coordinate the interdisciplinary team (RT, dietitian, social work/case management, home health, DME, pharmacy), arrange equipment/supplies/home nursing/prescriptions/follow-up before discharge, provide a written care plan and emergency/contingency instructions, teach oxygen fire safety with EMS/utility notification, reconcile medications, hand off to the PCP, coordinate school/community resources, and address transportation and financial barriers.
providerOrders: Coordinate interdisciplinary discharge planning (RT, dietitian, social work, home health, DME, pharmacy)
Train at least two caregivers; verify competency with return demonstration
Arrange home equipment, supplies, home nursing, prescriptions, and follow-up
Provide a written care plan and emergency/contingency instructions; teach oxygen fire safety
Reconcile medications; warm hand-off to the PCP; coordinate school/community resources

opts.text: A medically complex child needs coordinated, family-centered discharge planning
Parents/caregivers are partners and must be trained before discharge
At least two competent caregivers should be trained for home care
An interdisciplinary team (RT, dietitian, social work, home health) is involved
Verbal instructions alone are enough for complex home care
A written emergency/contingency plan is essential
Caregiver competency is verified with teach-back and return demonstration
Durable medical equipment and home health must be arranged before discharge
Discharge planning begins early, not at the last minute
Home oxygen requires fire-safety teaching and EMS/utility notification
Financial and transportation barriers can be ignored if teaching is done
Continuity includes follow-up, PCP hand-off, and medication reconciliation
Assess caregiver readiness, the home environment, and resources/barriers
Provide hands-on training with teach-back and return demonstration
Arrange DME, home health, prescriptions, and follow-up
Give a written care plan and emergency/contingency instructions
Complete a warm hand-off, reconcile meds, confirm readiness; document

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Does the action support a safe transition?

opts.text: Verify caregiver competency with return demonstration before discharge
Ensure at least two trained caregivers
Arrange DME (feeding pump, oxygen) and home health nursing
Provide a written emergency/contingency plan
Coordinate follow-up, PCP hand-off, and medication reconciliation
Notify EMS/utility company and teach oxygen fire safety
Discharge after only a verbal explanation, with no return demonstration
Send the child home before equipment and home health are arranged
Ignore the family's transportation and financial barriers

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Confirm at least two caregivers can safely perform the care (return demo)
Verify equipment, supplies, and home health are in place
Review the written emergency plan and when to call
Coordinate follow-up, PCP/school hand-off, and medication reconciliation
Document readiness, provide contact resources, and discharge
Caregivers safely manage the G-tube and oxygen at home
Home health visits and follow-up keep the plan on track
The family knows the emergency plan and calls appropriately
Nutrition and growth improve with reliable tube feeds
The tube dislodges and the family doesn't know what to do
Equipment never arrived, so feeds/oxygen are interrupted at home
A readmission occurs because teaching was rushed and unverified
An oxygen fire-safety lapse creates a home hazard
